results.possibleIdentity: Barbara Jones-Hogu (1938–2017) was a prominent American artist, painter, and printmaker who was a founding member of the influential African American artist collective AfriCOBRA (African Commune of Bad Relevant Artists), established in 1968 in Chicago. Her work, particularly her 1971 screenprint "Unite," became a central image of the Black Arts Movement. She was active throughout the late 1960s and 1970s, focusing on social and political themes through a "Black aesthetic." While she is closely associated with "Experimental Art" (the subtitle of a major publication on AfriCOBRA is "Experimental Art toward a School of Thought"), her direct association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is less documented, though she operated within the same experimental and technologically-influenced printmaking circles of that era.

Barbara Jones (1912–1978) was an English artist, writer, and muralist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She was a major figure in the appreciation of British popular and vernacular art, organizing the landmark exhibition "Black Eyes and Lemonade" (1951) and publishing the influential book "The Unsophisticated Arts." Her work as a graphic designer and muralist often intersected with discussions on industrial design and the aesthetic of mass production, themes that resonated with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). She remained active as an artist and writer until her death in 1978.

results.possibleIdentity: Thelma R. Newman (often spelled 'Newman' in historical records) was a prominent artist, educator, and author active in the late 1960s and 1970s who pioneered the use of industrial and synthetic materials in art. She is best known for her technical manuals that served as foundational texts for artists interested in merging art with new technologies and materials, such as 'Plastics as an Art Form' (1964) and 'Wax as Art Form' (1966). Her advocacy for artists to engage with scientific innovation and industrial processes aligns closely with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), and she is frequently cited in academic contexts alongside the organization's projects, such as the E.A.T. Pavilion. As an artist, her work often explored the malleable and translucent properties of plastics; notable works include 'Metamorphosis of a Human' (1961) and 'Surrogate Mother' (c. 1961). Professionally, she served as the Director of Art for the Union Township school system in New Jersey and was a frequent lecturer on the integration of new materials like polyurethane and epoxy resins into sculptural practice.
